content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. Demographic decline through 2040 will force school-network consolidation and closure of small selective tracks independent of policy choice, but government's simultaneous use of score-threshold levers is accelerating selective-track contraction beyond what demographic decline alone would cause, concentrating remaining grammar-school slots among already-advantaged urban populations.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: Annual births in Hungary fell from approximately 125,000 in the early 1990s to 77,500 in 2024, with a further 7.1% decline to ~72,000 in 2025, representing the lowest recorded level since 1949 and forcing school-network consolidation decisions in the 2020s–2030s regardless of selection policy.

Primary general school (általános iskola) enrollment declined 17.3% over 20 years, from 859,000 pupils in 2005/2006 to 710,000 in 2024/2025, tracking the shrinking birth cohorts directly, with first-grade intake falling 1.9% year-on-year in 2024/2025 to 95,000.
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: The net causal effect of early selection (6/8-year grammar-school entry at age 10–12) on individual student achievement and social mobility is not directly measured in Hungarian evidence; test-score gaps at selection reflect composition, not school value-added.

The relationship between consolidation-induced school closure, teacher workforce redeployment, and pedagogical quality in surviving small schools is unknown; closing small grammar schools may displace experienced teachers or may improve efficiency.
